summary: Hydrofluoric acid causes liquefactive necrosis due to fluoride ion interference with cell function, avoiding classic coagulative necrosis patterns; thus, it meets the criteria specified in the question without showing coagulation necrosis upon contact. Other strong acids typically cause coagulative necrosis but do not share these properties with HF.
